objectives: Conjugate high-frequency irregular preterite verbs: ser/ir, tener, estar, hacer, poder, querer, venir
Recognize the u-stem and j-stem preterite patterns
Use irregular preterites in natural conversation
Avoid common errors with ser vs ir in preterite

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Rebeca
dialogue_blocks.text: ¿Pudiste llegar a tiempo a la reunión?
dialogue_blocks.translation: Were you able to arrive on time for the meeting?
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [puˈðiste ʝeˈɣar a ˈtjempo a la rewˈnjon]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Diego
dialogue_blocks.text: No, tuve mucho tráfico y llegué tarde. Lo siento.
dialogue_blocks.translation: No, I had a lot of traffic and arrived late. I'm sorry.
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [no ‖ ˈtuβe ˈmuʧo ˈtɾafiko i ʝeˈɣe ˈtaɾðe ‖ lo ˈsjento]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Rebeca
dialogue_blocks.text: No te preocupes. ¿Y cómo estuvo la reunión?
dialogue_blocks.translation: Don't worry. And how was the meeting?
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [no te pɾeoˈkupes ‖ i ˈkomo esˈtuβo la rewˈnjon]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Diego
dialogue_blocks.text: Fue muy productiva. El jefe quiso presentar el nuevo proyecto.
dialogue_blocks.translation: It was very productive. The boss wanted to present the new project.
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [ˈfwe muj pɾoðukˈtiβa ‖ el ˈxefe ˈkiso pɾesenˈtaɾ el ˈnweβo pɾoˈʝekto]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Diego
dialogue_blocks.text: Lo hizo Mariana. Vino con muchas ideas y estuvo genial.
dialogue_blocks.translation: Mariana did it. She came with lots of ideas and was great.
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [lo ˈiθo maˈɾjana ‖ ˈβino kon ˈmuʧas iˈðeas i esˈtuβo xeˈnjal]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Rebeca
dialogue_blocks.text: ¡Qué bueno! ¿Y adónde fueron a comer después?
dialogue_blocks.translation: Great! And where did you go to eat afterwards?
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [ke ˈβweno ‖ i aˈðonde ˈfweron a koˈmeɾ desˈpwes]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Diego
dialogue_blocks.text: Fuimos al restaurante de la esquina. La comida estuvo riquísima.
dialogue_blocks.translation: We went to the restaurant on the corner. The food was delicious.
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [ˈfwimos al restawˈɾante ðe la esˈkina ‖ la koˈmiða esˈtuβo riˈkisima]

exercises.instruction: Escoge la forma correcta del pretérito irregular (Choose the correct irregular preterite)
exercises.question: Yesterday I had to work all day. Which is correct?

options.text: Ayer tuve que trabajar todo el día.
Ayer tenía que trabajar todo el día.
Ayer tienes que trabajar todo el día.
Ayer tendré que trabajar todo el día.

feedback.correct: ¡Correcto! 'Tuve' is the preterite of tener — used here for a completed obligation.
feedback.incorrect: 'Tuve' (preterite of tener) is correct for a completed past event. 'Tenía' is imperfect, 'tienes' is present, 'tendré' is future.

exercises.instruction: Une el infinitivo con su pretérito irregular (Match verb to its irregular preterite — yo form)
Completa el diálogo con el pretérito irregular correcto (Complete with the irregular preterite)

dialogue.speaker: Jorge
dialogue.text: ¡_____ increíble! _____ a la playa todos los días. (ser / ir)

dialogue.speaker: Jorge
dialogue.text: _____ los mejores tacos de mi vida. (tener)

speaking_rubric.title: Cuéntame qué pasó (Tell me what happened)
speaking_rubric.scenario: Something memorable happened to you recently — a trip, an event, a challenge. Tell your friend using at least 4 irregular preterite verbs.
speaking_rubric.prompt: Usa verbos irregulares en pretérito para contar algo que te pasó.

sample_response.text: El mes pasado fui a Guadalajara por trabajo. Tuve una reunión importante y todo estuvo muy bien. Hice nuevos amigos y al final fuimos a cenar juntos.
sample_response.ipa: [el mes paˈsaðo ˈfwi a ɣwaðalaˈxaɾa ‖ ˈtuβe una rewˈnjon impoɾˈtante]

cultural_notes.formality: In Mexico, storytelling often uses dramatic expressions: '¡No lo puedo creer!' (I can't believe it!) or '¡Sí, de verdad!' (Yes, really!) to keep the listener engaged.
cultural_notes.gestures: Raising both hands palms-up while saying 'no pude' (I couldn't) is a common gesture expressing helplessness or frustration.
cultural_notes.regional_variations: Mexicans frequently use 'andar' in preterite idiomatically: '¿Cómo anduviste?' (How were you doing? / How did things go?). The verb 'andar' has an irregular preterite: anduve, anduviste, anduvo.
